retrieved_docs.title: New York State Infection Control -- Issues of Concern -- Element 5: Creation and Maintenance of a Safe Environment for Patient Care Through the Application of Infection Control Practices and Principles for Cleaning, Disinfection, and Sterilization
retrieved_docs.content: Sterilization: Chemical or physical destruction or removal of all pathogenic microorganisms and their spores from a surface. Effective sterilization techniques are essential, and failure could lead to significant morbidity and mortality. The most commonly employed techniques are as follows: Heat method: Heat is used to kill microbes. Wet heat/steam: Autoclaves use steam heated to 121 to 134 °C under pressure to kill or deactivate all microbes, bacterial spores, and viruses by hydrolysis and coagulation of cell proteins. Dry heat: Bacteria are exposed to high temperatures by flaming, incineration, or a hot air oven. This method is typically used for needles, scalpels, and scissors. Filtration: Filtering with a pore size that is too small for microbes to pass through is used to remove bacteria; however, it does not remove viruses or phages. Radiation: Exposing packed materials to radiation such as UV, X-rays, and gamma rays for sterilization. UV radiation is safer but has low penetration. X-rays are used for sterilizing large packages and pallet loads of medical devices. Gamma radiation has high penetration and is used to sterilize disposable medical equipment, such as syringes, needles, cannulas, and IV sets, as well as food. Chemical method: A reliable method to kill microbes, but it can damage the material to be sterilized. Gas: Gases such as ethylene oxide and carbon dioxide penetrate quickly into the material, similar to steam. However, they are expensive and may pose explosion risks.

retrieved_docs.title: New York State Infection Control -- Issues of Concern -- Element 5: Creation and Maintenance of a Safe Environment for Patient Care Through the Application of Infection Control Practices and Principles for Cleaning, Disinfection, and Sterilization
retrieved_docs.content: Reprocessing Procedures: Health professionals should be aware of the OSHA and CDC guidelines, which utilize the Spaulding classification system, depending on the need for disinfection or sterilization, and categorize items as critical, semicritical, or noncritical. [27] Recommendations for cleaning, disinfection, and sterilization vary depending on several factors: The presence of lumens, hinges, or crevices that may be sources for internal or external contamination The material composition and design of the equipment The frequency of contact of the instrument with patient surfaces and operators' hands The types and numbers of microorganisms to which they are exposed

retrieved_docs.title: Tubal Sterilization -- Technique or Treatment -- Open Approaches
retrieved_docs.content: Tubal sterilization can also be accomplished via an open approach. If the patient undergoes a cesarean delivery, tubal sterilization is performed immediately following hysterotomy closure and before closing the abdominal incision. Following a vaginal delivery, tubal sterilization is typically accomplished through a mini-laparotomy under regional anesthesia. Additionally, a mini-laparotomy may be used in patients with significant risks for laparoscopy and in settings with limited laparoscopic resources. [24]

retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: New York State Infection Control -- Issues of Concern -- Element 5: Creation and Maintenance of a Safe Environment for Patient Care Through the Application of Infection Control Practices and Principles for Cleaning, Disinfection, and Sterilization
retrieved_docs.content: Semicritical items come in contact with mucous membranes and nonintact skin. Common examples include oral thermometers, nasopharyngoscopes, and vaginal specula. Semicritical items require high-level chemical disinfectants that are cleared by the Food and Drug Administration, such as hydrogen peroxide, glutaraldehyde, ortho-phthalaldehyde, or peracetic acid with hydrogen peroxide. Some semicritical medical equipment may have challenges due to potential damage to the instrument. Staff responsible for disinfecting this equipment should be trained and competency tested in safe and proper reprocessing techniques. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: New York State Infection Control -- Issues of Concern -- Reprocessing Effectiveness:
retrieved_docs.content: The effectiveness of reprocessing medical equipment, devices, and instruments depends on multiple factors: Prior cleaning and removal of organic matter and biofilms Selection and use of disinfectants and sterilization techniques Monitoring the activity and stability of disinfectants, contact time, and usage Proper handling, packaging, and storage post-disinfection or sterilization

retrieved_docs.title: New York State Infection Control -- Issues of Concern -- Element 5: Creation and Maintenance of a Safe Environment for Patient Care Through the Application of Infection Control Practices and Principles for Cleaning, Disinfection, and Sterilization
retrieved_docs.content: Critical items directly or indirectly enter sterile tissue or the vascular system. Common examples include surgical instruments, duodenoscopes, injection needles, or cardiac catheters. These items must be sterile before coming in contact with tissue and should be cleaned to remove debris, followed by sterilization. Steam sterilization is preferred where possible; however, heat-sensitive materials may be sterilized using ethylene oxide, hydrogen peroxide gas plasma, vaporized hydrogen peroxide, hydrogen peroxide with ozone, or liquid chemical sterilants if other sterilizing methods are not possible. [36] Prions are unusually resistant to decontamination and sterilization procedures. [36] Consultation with an infection control specialist is essential for guidance on cleaning and disinfecting when prion disease, such as Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease, is confirmed or suspected.

retrieved_docs.title: New York State Infection Control -- Issues of Concern -- Element 5: Creation and Maintenance of a Safe Environment for Patient Care Through the Application of Infection Control Practices and Principles for Cleaning, Disinfection, and Sterilization
retrieved_docs.content: Pre-cleaning is the first step of reprocessing. This critical step prepares medical equipment, devices, and instruments for reprocessing by removing visible soil, debris, and liquids from internal and external surfaces. This step should be done as soon as possible after use, and the cleaning solutions should be changed regularly. The choice of cleaning, disinfection, or sterilization method should be guided by the equipment's intended use and the manufacturer's instructions.

retrieved_docs.title: Tubal Sterilization -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Although rare, post-sterilization pregnancy can occur. The cumulative 10-year failure rate of tubal sterilization using traditional occlusive methods or postpartum partial salpingectomy ranges from 7.5 to 54.3 pregnancies per 1,000 sterilization procedures, depending on the technique used and the age of the patient at sterilization, with younger ages being associated with higher rates of contraceptive failure. [14] Of note, data on the long-term failure rates of complete bilateral salpingectomy are not yet available, but rates should theoretically approach zero. If post-sterilization pregnancy occurs, there is a relatively high risk of a resulting ectopic pregnancy. [15] As with any surgical procedure, other procedural risks include bleeding, infection, injury to nearby organs, and wound and anesthesia complications. [16] Therefore, due to the importance of permanent sterilization on women's health, healthcare professionals should recognize the indications and contraindications for tubal sterilization; the risks, benefits, and complications of the procedure; the techniques available to perform this mode of sterilization; and the role of the interprofessional team in caring for patients who undergo the type of surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: New York State Infection Control -- Issues of Concern -- Reprocessing Effectiveness:
retrieved_docs.content: All items must be cleaned to eliminate organic material before disinfection or sterilization. Disinfectants and steriliants must be applied for sufficient durations to achieve full effectiveness. The recommended contact time for low-level disinfectants is at least 1 minute. In contrast, the exposure time for most high-level disinfectants ranges from 8 to 45 minutes, and the temperature typically varies from 20 to 25 °C. Most liquid chemical sterilants have exposure times ranging from 3 to 12 hours, except for 0.2% peracetic acid, which has an exposure time of 12 minutes at 50 to 56 °C. In addition, these sterilants must be administered at the recommended concentration, pH, and temperature. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Tubal Sterilization -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications and contraindications for tubal sterilization, and know how to effectively counsel patients regarding the procedure. Implement the proper techniques for performing tubal sterilization. Identify the complications of tubal sterilization. Employ interprofessional team strategies for optimizing care coordination and communication to optimize patient outcomes after tubal sterilization. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: New York State Infection Control -- Issues of Concern -- Reprocessing Effectiveness:
retrieved_docs.content: Expectations of Healthcare Professionals: Healthcare professionals working in settings where cleaning and reprocessing of medical equipment is performed separately from patient care areas should understand Standard and Universal Precautions and the core concepts and principles of cleaning, disinfection, and sterilization, and follow recommended safe practices for handling sterile instruments, equipment, and devices in medical settings. These professionals should also verify and follow their local processes for handling and preparing used equipment before submission for reprocessing, such as pre-cleaning or soaking.

retrieved_docs.title: New York State Infection Control -- Issues of Concern -- Element 5: Creation and Maintenance of a Safe Environment for Patient Care Through the Application of Infection Control Practices and Principles for Cleaning, Disinfection, and Sterilization
retrieved_docs.content: Decontamination: A process of using chemicals or physical techniques to destroy, inactivate, or remove bloodborne pathogens on a surface.

retrieved_docs.title: Sterile Technique -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: To open the instrument trays, ensure there is a plastic lock that identifies an indicator change, a label with the Julian date and load number, and make sure it is the correct set for the procedure. Look at the outside container for moisture or condensation; if there is moisture, do not use it and retrieve another set. If there is no condensation, proceed by grasping the latches and flipping them open, automatically breaking the plastic locks. Lift the lid straight up and step back before flipping the lid over; this ensures that dust or particles don't fall into the tray. Once you flip the lid over, check for moisture and remove the paper filter. To check the filter for any holes, lift it toward a light to ensure no pin-size holes. If the filter looks good, carefully look into the pan for an indicator; do not lean over the pan. If you cannot see an indicator, look more closely after being gowned and gloved. Until you are scrubbed in, the instruments are complete; once you are gowned and gloved, carefully pick up the inside basket and hold it in front of you until the circulating nurse checks the bottom filter and the bottom of the tray for moisture. This is also an excellent time to find the inside indicator. If you cannot find an indicator, do not use the tray. Once the nurse verifies the instrument tray and you see an indicator, you can place the instruments on the back table.

retrieved_docs.title: New York State Infection Control -- Issues of Concern -- Element 5: Creation and Maintenance of a Safe Environment for Patient Care Through the Application of Infection Control Practices and Principles for Cleaning, Disinfection, and Sterilization
retrieved_docs.content: Healthcare professionals should understand the principles of infection control, including cleaning, disinfection, and sterilization. The following definitions provide a foundational understanding of the concepts. [36] Contamination: The presence of microorganisms on a surface. Cleaning: Scrubbing of visible soil and foreign materials from objects and surfaces using water, soaps, detergents, or enzymatic products. Disinfection: A process of using chemicals or wet pasteurization to eliminate pathogenic microorganisms. Low-level disinfectants destroy all vegetative bacteria but not bacterial spores and are classified as disinfectants. Intermediate-level disinfectants destroy all vegetative bacteria but not bacterial spores. These agents are classified as tuberculocidal. High-level disinfectants kill all organisms except bacterial spores and are approved as chemical germicide sterilants.

retrieved_docs.title: New York State Infection Control -- Issues of Concern -- Element 5: Creation and Maintenance of a Safe Environment for Patient Care Through the Application of Infection Control Practices and Principles for Cleaning, Disinfection, and Sterilization
retrieved_docs.content: The manufacturer's guidelines should always be referred to ensure that the selected reprocessing method is compatible with the equipment's materials and the recommended time, temperature, and pressure for reprocessing.

The infectious process is identified as the underlying mechanism of post‑abortion complications, linking infection directly to adverse outcomes after termination procedures. Collectively, this structured framework interrelates prenatal surveillance, invasive diagnostics, therapeutic interventions, delivery assistance, placental and ectopic‑pregnancy management, and post‑delivery care, providing a comprehensive reference for clinical documentation, education, and patient‑centered obstetric management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 207
retrieved_docs.content: Female infertility is mapped to the female reproductive organs and associated with amenorrhea (غِيَابُ الإِباضَة), emphasizing infertility as a manifestation of underlying reproductive physiology. Infectious processes are identified as the pathological basis of post‑abortion complications, underscoring infection control in post‑abortion care (إجْهاضٌ مَصْحُوبٌ بعَدْوَى). Laparoscopic treatment of ectopic pregnancy without salpingectomy or oophorectomy illustrates conservative minimally invasive management of ectopic and abdominal pregnancies and remains linked to fetal monitoring, reflecting continued fetal assessment. Postpartum obstetric operations are associated with immediate postpartum care and investigation (Zorg en onderzoek onmiddellijk na bevalling). Collectively, إجراء توليدي serves as the central hierarchical category that integrates ante‑ and postpartum obstetric interventions—from diagnostic punctures and fetal monitoring to major surgical operations—within a framework

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 200
retrieved_docs.content: that a wide range of interventions target this anatomy. “Female reproductive function” is related to “female infertility,” which is interpreted through fertility assessment and mapped to amenorrhea (غِيَابُ الإِباضَة), highlighting the clinical association between absent menstruation and difficulty conceiving. INFECTIOUS PROCESS is identified as a pathological process that can give rise to postabortion complications, situating infection as a key contributor to adverse outcomes after an abortion; post‑abortion complications are also located in the female reproductive organs, sharing an anatomical site with infertility and dyspareunia. Forceps delivery is recognized as a specific obstetric procedure within the broader category of delivery methods. Overall, the integrated network demonstrates how umbilical cord complications, delivery modalities, infectious pathology, and reproductive health conditions such as dyspareunia, infertility, and post‑abortion complications are interrelated

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2535
retrieved_docs.content: Female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) and endometrial implantation (انتباذ بطاني رحمي) are interconnected medical conditions. Prevention and control measures are applicable to both female infertility and endometrial implantation, and the two conditions are related, indicating that endometrial implantation can contribute to or be associated with female infertility and that managing endometrial pathology is important in the care of women with infertility.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 521
retrieved_docs.content: network of gynecologic and endocrine surgical procedures, their anatomical targets, and the hormonal systems that contextualize them, providing clinicians with a structured understanding of how specific ovarian, tubal, and endocrine interventions are interrelated within medical taxonomy and patient care planning.

Collectively, the integrated knowledge of urogenital anatomy, renal and prostate pathology, the full spectrum of female reproductive disorders, laparoscopic and peritoneal surgical techniques, extracorporeal lithotripsy, sexual dysfunction categories, and targeted pharmacologic therapies illustrates a comprehensive framework for patient assessment and multidisciplinary care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 205

Vaginal Assisted Delivery is the parent of instrumental delivery (الولادة بالملقط), which in turn links to its possible failure outcome (فشل التوليد بالملقط). Post‑partum obstetric operation connects to immediate post‑delivery care and investigation (Zorg en onderzoek onmiddellijk na bevalling), emphasizing rapid assessment after birth. Anatomical context is provided by the inverse‑ISA relationship between Procedure by site and Procedure on organ, situating Female genital and obstetric procedures within the broader domain of female genital organs (ženské pohlavní orgány). Dyspareunia of non‑psychogenic origin and female infertility (Arabic غِيَابُ الإِباضَة, “absence of menstruation”) are localized to these organs, with infertility positioned within female reproductive function. The infectious process is identified as the underlying mechanism of post‑abortion

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 188
retrieved_docs.content: site for major vascular interventions such as resection, replacement, and anastomosis, while the radius is notable for its blood supply, potential involvement in transplantation, and susceptibility to injury. Alterations in vascular supply to the radius and to the female reproductive organs are qualified by the presence of cervical tumors (ورم عنق الرحم) or vulvar tumors (ورم فرجي). Female infertility, described in Arabic as غِيَابُ الإِباضَة (absence of menstruation), underscores the necessity of evaluating menstrual function and blood‑supply integrity when determining a woman’s reproductive potential. Post‑abortion complications are localized to the female reproductive organs, indicating that adverse events after abortion affect the same anatomical structures implicated in infertility. Consequently, comprehensive gynecologic assessment and targeted interventions are required to address menstrual irregularities, preserve organ integrity, and manage the interconnected vascular,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1860
retrieved_docs.content: The FDA Center For Devices and Radiological Health Terminology includes العقم (infertility) as a concept within its subset, highlighting the regulatory relevance of reproductive health terms. Intrauterine pregnancy is clinically associated with other excision of cervix and uterus, indicating that surgical removal or partial removal of cervical or uterine tissue can occur in the context of pregnancy management or its complications. Intrauterine pregnancy is also clinically associated with العقم (infertility); this relationship reflects that certain uterine or cervical surgeries, as well as complications arising during or after pregnancy, can impair future fertility. Additionally, Intrauterine pregnancy is clinically associated with الْتِهابُ بِطانَةِ الرَّحِمِ (endometritis), an inflammation of the uterine lining that can develop during pregnancy and may contribute to both immediate maternal morbidity and longer‑term reproductive sequelae such as infertility. Recognizing how intrauterine pregnancy, surgical excision of cervical/uterine tissue, endometritis, and infertility interrelate is important for patient care planning, risk assessment, and the development of medical devices and therapies regulated by the FDA.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 1102
retrieved_docs.content: The collection of gynecologic interventions is organized under the overarching category “عملية للأعضاء التناسلية الأنثوية” (procedures for female genital organs). Within this category, major surgical procedures include “إزالة الرحم” (hysterectomy) and “استئصال المبيض” (ovarian removal), as well as “توسيع الرحم وكشطه” (uterine dilation and curettage). “توسيع الرحم وكشطه” further comprises specific techniques such as “Dilatation and curettage (D&C); aspiration after delivery or abortion” and its child procedure “شفط بالتخلية” (suction curettage). The procedure “Aspiration curettage following delivery or abortion” is classified as “Dilatation and curettage (D&C); aspiration after delivery or abortion,” indicating that it is a particular form of D&C performed after delivery or abortion. This hierarchical organization clarifies how various gynecologic surgeries are related, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate interventions and understanding their place within comprehensive female reproductive care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 188
retrieved_docs.content: Consequently, comprehensive gynecologic assessment and targeted interventions are required to address menstrual irregularities, preserve organ integrity, and manage the interconnected vascular, structural, and hormonal factors influencing female reproductive health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1920
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical repair and revision of anastomosis is classified as an “other or therapeutic procedure; female organs,” indicating that this type of operative intervention is used to correct or modify anastomotic connections within the female reproductive system and is grouped under therapeutic procedures targeting female organ pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1483
retrieved_docs.content: Operation to facilitate delivery is defined as الولادة القيصرية (Cesarean section). الولادة القيصرية is clinically associated with إنهاء الحمل - اختياري (optional termination of pregnancy) and with Intrauterine pregnancy. In clinical practice, الولادة القيصرية frequently co‑occurs with ربط البوق (tubal ligation). Intrauterine pregnancy is also clinically associated with الْتِهابُ بِطانَةِ الرَّحِمِ (endometritis) and with ربط البوق (tubal ligation). These relationships highlight that Cesarean delivery, optional pregnancy termination, intrauterine gestation, tubal ligation, and endometritis are interlinked considerations in obstetric care, influencing surgical planning, infection risk management, and decisions about concurrent sterilization procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 422
retrieved_docs.content: Intrauterine pregnancy is clinically associated with a spectrum of other complications of pregnancy, which are located in the uterus. These other complications of pregnancy are also clinically linked to elective termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري). Intrauterine pregnancy further shows clinical associations with cesarean delivery (الولادة القيصرية), endometritis (الْتِهابُ بِطانَةِ الرَّحِمِ), and tubal ligation (ربط البوق). Cesarean delivery is defined as an operation to facilitate delivery and its direct procedural site is the uterus. Cesarean delivery frequently co‑occurs with tubal ligation, reflecting the common practice of performing sterilization at the time of a C‑section. Elective termination of pregnancy is also clinically associated with cesarean delivery, indicating that surgical termination may be employed when indicated. Vacuum dilatation of the cervix is a child procedure of the operation to facilitate delivery and is classified as a procedure categorized by the device involved. Together, these relationships illustrate how intrauterine pregnancy, its potential complications, and various obstetric surgical interventions are interrelated, informing comprehensive clinical decision‑making and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 552
retrieved_docs.content: Infertility in women of cervical origin is classified as a specific type of cervical disorder and is also considered a subset of female infertility. Both cervical disorders and female infertility are targets for prevention and control strategies, as are peptic ulcers and endometriosis. Endometriosis is linked to female infertility, indicating that the presence of endometrial implants can contribute to difficulties conceiving. Pharmacologic management of endometriosis may include danazol, which has the potential to treat this condition. Implementing preventive and control measures for cervical disorders, peptic ulcers, female infertility, and endometriosis is essential for reducing disease burden and improving reproductive health outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: & Gynecology also encompasses related surgical procedures such as salpingo‑oophorectomy (complete or partial, unilateral or bilateral). The concept of “Organ” connects these procedures to their anatomical targets, linking the excised tissues (e.g., vaginal biopsy, fine‑needle ovarian biopsy) to the organs themselves and to endoscopic access methods (pelvic endoscopy, uterine hysteroscopy). This integrated hierarchy provides a systematic framework for the histopathological evaluation of gynecologic and ovarian tissues, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate diagnostic approaches and ensuring accurate tissue sampling within obstetric‑gynecologic care.
